Welcome back to Wall Power. I=E2=80=99m Marion
Maneker.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

The London auction season can often set the tone for the U.S. And given the=
 two-year downturn in art auctions=E2=80=94not to mention the uncertain pol=
itical and economic environment in America=E2=80=94this year=E2=80=99s sale=
s may receive even more attention than normal. With that in mind, I dug int=
o the sales to find the lots that would be most interesting to you.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

But before we get there=E2=80=A6

Julie Brener Davich
Julie Brener Davich

=E2=80=8A

Gallery Hopping With Julie
--------------------------

Kelsey Leonard, my former Sotheby=E2=80=99s colleague,
texted me last week saying that I had to get over to Nino Mier Gallery, in =
Tribeca, to check out the show of recent paintings by Deborah Druick. =E2=
=80=9CFun show downtown. Very nouveau Chicago Imagist (i.e. Julie Curtiss)=
=E2=80=94feels hyper commercial!=E2=80=9D she wrote. I trust Kelsey=E2=80=
=99s eye, so off I went.=C2=A0

The works in Past Present Tense could be described as feminist, but Druick=
=E2=80=99s characters, reminiscent of 1960s
housewives=E2=80=94faceless women in domestic settings, with =E2=80=9Ctopia=
ry hair,=E2=80=9D as Druick has described it=E2=80=94are not liberated: The=
y are trapped within painted frames, bound by the edges of the canvas. The =
compositions themselves are highly decorative, with playful dimensionality =
and clashing patterns=E2=80=94a hint of a Gucci print here, a Prada print t=
here. Druick achieves her fine lines and matte finish by using acrylic and =
flashe, a vinyl-based paint. Her favorite brush has only two bristles.

Installation view of Deborah Druick, Past Present Tense. Courtesy Nino Mier=
 Gallery.

Installation view of Deborah Druick, Past Present Tense. Courtesy of Nino M=
ier Gallery.

The artist draws upon her upbringing in a conservative neighborhood
in Montreal, where women were expected to take on traditional roles and had=
 few ways of expressing themselves outside of their hairstyles and clothing=
=2E =E2=80=9CIt was terrifying to me=
,=E2=80=9D she told me. =E2=80=9CThat was=
 not what I thought life was all about.=E2=80=9D One painting, titled Aspir=
ational (2025), depicts a perfectly coiffed woman in a =E2=80=9Cchurch hat=
=E2=80=9D subtly patterned with blue eyes and eyelashes=E2=80=94her communi=
ty=E2=80=99s ideal of beauty.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

After finishing art school in Montreal, Druick moved to Toronto to be a cre=
ative director for a retail company designing window displays and publicati=
ons, then was headhunted for a job in Hong Kong, where she stayed for a dec=
ade. The influence of both her retail career and her time in Asia is eviden=
t in her work, particularly the influence of Japanese woodblock prints of c=
ourtesans. To wit: Her painting Plumage (2023) was inspired by a visit to a=
n aviary in Hong Kong,
and seeing how birds use their plumage to attract mates.

Druick, who did not begin painting full-time until age 64, has spoken about=
 the influence of the Chicago Imagists on her work, as Kelsey identified, a=
s well as surrealism. In Effacing (2024), an outstretched hand is held unde=
r running water, but the reflection in the mirror above it cannot belong to=
 that same person=E2=80=94so who is it? =E2=80=9CThe works feel vintage, bu=
t fresh at the same time,=E2=80=9D the gallery=E2=80=99s sales
director, Maya Code-Williams, told me. The paintings, which range in price =
from $14,000 to $38,000, are on view through March 22.

 the same consignor. Meanwhile, Ernst=E2=80=99s beguiling Coloradeau de M=
=C3=A9duse, from ( https://www.christies.com/en/lot/lot-6523644?utm_campaig=
n=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SU=
BSCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.=
io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) 1953, is being offered with a =C2=A3700,=
000 estimate. It has never been offered publicly before.

The Pride of Phillips
---------------------

Phillips, which prides itself on making markets for artists who have recent=
ly
emerged on the primary market, leads off with a painting ( https://www.phil=
lips.com/detail/ding-shilun/UK010125/1?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIB=
ERS+%283%2F2%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%2=
9&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da4=
2abd12a ) by Ding Shilun, who just had a prominent show at the ICA Miami. T=
here=E2=80=99s also a work by Florian Krewer, who studied with Peter Doig a=
nd Nathana=C3=ABlle Herbelin, the latter of whom had a show at Mus=C3=A9e d=
=E2=80=99Orsay last year.=C2=A0

Phillips=E2=80=99s
top lot, meanwhile, is a Joan Mitchell painting ( https://www.phillips.com/=
detail/joan-mitchell/UK010125/9?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%28=
3%2F2%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%29&utm_m=
edium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a=
 ) from 1975, Canada II, which is estimated at =C2=A33 million and has neve=
r been on the auction market before. Basquiat=E2=80=99s 1984 painting ( htt=
ps://www.phillips.com/detail/jeanmichel-basquiat/UK010125/18?utm_campaign=
=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUB=
SCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.i=
o&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) Pattya is estimated at =C2=A32 million. T=
he
house has two Christopher Wool works on offer. One, Lester=E2=80=99s Sister=
 (My Brain), from 2000, plays with Wool=E2=80=99s themes of mechanical repr=
oduction and gestural abstraction; it=E2=80=99s backed by a guarantor at =
=C2=A31.2 million. The other ( https://www.phillips.com/detail/christopher-=
wool/UK010125/6?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%29&ut=
m_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_ac=
tion&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ), one of the f=
ew small word paintings ever to come to auction, with the phrase =E2=80=9CY=
ou Make Me,=E2=80=9D is estimated at =C2=A3600,000. There=E2=80=99s
also an early 1931 painting by Le Corbusier, estimated ( https://www.philli=
ps.com/detail/le-corbusier/UK010125/13?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIB=
ERS+%283%2F2%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%2=
9&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da4=
2abd12a ) at =C2=A3950,000.=C2=A0

=E2=80=8A

Endnotes=E2=80=A6
-----------

The Trump/DOGE cuts have gotten so bad that staff inside
USAID are now lashing out at other agencies. It seems from this story ( htt=
ps://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2025/02/state-department-embassy-=
art-food-aid/681862/?utm_campaign=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%=
29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F2%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Dema=
il_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) in The A=
tlantic that someone inside the State Department is mad that so many life-s=
aving and important USAID programs got cut even as the State=E2=80=99s long=
-standing and vaunted Art in Embassies program appeared at first to be movi=
ng ahead on schedule. So they got
in touch with Laura Barr=C3=B3n-L=C3=B3pez to complain.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

The Art in Embassies program has been going on for decades. It=E2=80=99s an=
 extension of broader American efforts carried out by the State Department=
=E2=80=94and even the C.I.A.=E2=80=94to use American modern art, particular=
ly abstract expressionism, to convince citizens of other nations of America=
n cultural superiority and freedom. You cannot blame the good
people at USAID, who are under attack for simply having done their jobs cre=
ating humanitarian programs, for resenting a feckless administration that a=
ppeared initially to have spared a program to decorate embassies at the sam=
e time they were cutting food aid.

I=E2=80=99m not sure we should be rooting for the art purchases to be halte=
d. But it really isn=E2=80=99t clear from Barr=C3=B3n-L=C3=B3pez=E2=80=99s =
story what the actual status of the program is now. She quotes a spokespers=
on at State saying
the program was suspended, but that person could provide no concrete detail=
s. That said, the long-term value of the Art in Embassies program should be=
 evaluated in a proper process. It certainly should not be blamed for Elon =
Musk=E2=80=99s attacks on the rest of the State Department and USAID.

=E2=80=8A

We can all contemplate while we wait to be in touch again on Tuesday. Let=
=E2=80=99s
speak then.

style=3D"margin:0;"><br/>The works in <em>Past Present Tense</em> could be =

s

ry hair,=E2=80=9D as Druick has described it=E2=80=94are not liberated: The=
y are trapped within painted frames, bound by the edges of the canvas. The =
compositions themselves are highly decorative, with playful dimensionality =
and clashing patterns=E2=80=94a hint of a Gucci print here, a Prada print t=
here. Druick achieves her fine lines and matte finish by using acrylic and =
flashe, a vinyl-based paint. Her favorite brush has only two bristles.</p><=

0;margin-bottom:0px;">After finishing art school in Montreal, Druick moved =

e she stayed for a decade. The influence of both her retail career and her =
time in Asia is evident in her work, particularly the influence of Japanese=
 woodblock prints of courtesans. To wit: Her painting <em>Plumage</em> (202=
3) was inspired by a visit to an aviary in Hong Kong,
and seeing how birds use their plumage to attract mates.</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin:0;"><br/>Druick, who did not begin painting full-time until age 64, has=
 spoken about the influence of the Chicago Imagists on her work, as Kelsey =

ched hand is held under running water, but the reflection in the mirror abo=
ve it cannot belong to that same person=E2=80=94so who is it? =E2=80=9CThe =
works feel vintage, but fresh at the same time,=E2=80=9D the gallery=E2=80=
=99s sales
director, <strong>Maya Code-Williams</strong>, told me. The paintings, whic=
h range in price from $14,000 to $38,000, are on view through March 22.</p>=
